lesson_contract.objective: Prepare and present an offer as a clear next step while protecting accuracy, consent, and handoff quality.
objective_points: Prepare the information needed for an offer
Confirm the next action without pressure
lesson_contract.transition_spoken_text: Take the Closing and Deal Engineering checkpoint next. Then complete the Cadence and Close Capstone.

required_teaching_beats.description: Discovery, rapport, objection handling, and follow-through make formal agreement easier.
Closing formalizes a decision built through earlier conversations.
A complete, motivated, documented handoff makes offer review possible.
Property facts, comparable information, condition, and seller priorities feed offer preparation.
A price objection starts with acknowledgment and a question about the gap.
Negotiation seeks written terms both sides understand and accept.
Verbal agreement moves into documents, signatures, ownership review, and closing logistics.
Seller remorse often points back to a person who did not feel heard.
Family influence, title issues, and competing offers are common closing risks.
Discovery, empathy, notes, and follow-through affect the quality of the close.
The close directs the learner to the checkpoint and capstone.

required_examples.description: A seller says the offer is lower than expected.
A seller reconsiders after signing because the decision did not feel understood.
A family member raises a concern late in the process.
A lien or unclear ownership interrupts closing.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: you're not the person presenting the offer or negotiating the final terms
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Teach the work without assigning it to or excluding a named role.
Use current-process ownership language with no role title.
Describe the information the next step needs, not a named team.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: the acquisition team reviews your notes
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Describe review of the complete record without a named team.
Remove role-bound shirt text from replacement visuals.
Use a generic closing-logistics visual with no role title.

scenes.title: Closing begins earlier
scenes.spoken_text: Closing should feel like the formal next step in a conversation that has already been handled well. Rapport matters. Thorough discovery matters. So does the way you answered objections and followed through after the seller shared something difficult. If those pieces are solid, the written agreement does not arrive out of nowhere. It gives shape to a decision the seller has been considering throughout the process. That is why a rushed discovery question or a forgotten follow-up can show up again at the closing table. The formal document carries the facts and trust built before it.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a calm medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: Open on continuity from conversation to written agreement.

scenes.title: Build a complete handoff
scenes.spoken_text: A clean offer review starts with a complete record. Confirm the property facts, the seller's priorities, every person whose consent matters, and the questions still waiting for an answer. Write down the condition details and the reason the seller is considering a change. A thin note forces the next conversation to start over. A complete handoff lets the current process review the same situation the seller described.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a complete generic property record
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no role label on the recipient of the handoff.

scenes.title: Prepare the offer inputs
scenes.spoken_text: Bring together ownership information, property condition, comparable information used by the current process, the seller's price expectation, timing needs, and any special request. Mark what is confirmed and what still needs review. If a roof age is unknown, say that. If another person may have an ownership interest, flag it. An assumption should never drift into a draft number, a date, or a written responsibility.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea sorting confirmed and open-question cards
visual_plan.editor_note: No software branding or role titles.

scenes.title: Protect accuracy and authority
scenes.spoken_text: Know which parts of the process you can explain and which parts need review. A draft number is not a commitment. An estimated closing date is not the written date. A structure that worked somewhere else is not automatically available here. When the seller asks for an exception, document the request in their words. Explain what will happen next, and wait for the current written process to confirm the answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a confirmed and pending terms board
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no price or date on screen.

scenes.title: Present the written terms cleanly
scenes.spoken_text: When the written offer is ready, explain it in plain language. Walk through price, timing, conditions, costs, access, and the responsibilities assigned to each side. Ask the seller to stop whenever something is unclear. Do not rush past a clause because it feels familiar to you. The seller needs enough space to read, ask questions, and seek outside advice when appropriate. Understanding is the standard for the conversation.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea reviewing a generic written offer with a seller
visual_plan.editor_note: Show no signature pressure or countdown.

scenes.title: Work the price gap with questions
scenes.spoken_text: The seller may say, I was hoping for more. Start by acknowledging it: I hear you. What number were you expecting, and what does that number need to accomplish? Listen for the reason behind the gap. Then separate what the written offer already addresses from what may need another review. Do not argue that the seller is wrong. Find out whether there is a responsible path both sides can understand.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea practicing a calm price-gap question
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no currency figure or promised movement.

scenes.title: Confirm consent without pressure
scenes.spoken_text: A seller's yes needs to be informed and voluntary. Confirm who needs to sign and whether another person should hear the same explanation. Give the seller room to read the document. If the answer is no or not yet, record the unresolved issue and agree on the next action. Urgency belongs in the conversation only when a real, documented deadline exists. Pressure cannot substitute for consent.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea seated with an unsigned agreement and open space
visual_plan.editor_note: Avoid celebratory signature imagery before consent is confirmed.

scenes.title: Understand what follows agreement
scenes.spoken_text: After verbal agreement, the terms move into the written document and signatures. Then ownership, liens, access, condition items, required documents, and closing logistics still have to be coordinated. Responsibilities vary by transaction, so follow the current process and escalate a mismatch instead of guessing. Keep the seller informed about confirmed next steps. A signed document begins another part of the work; it does not erase open facts.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a document, ownership, access, and closing sequence
visual_plan.editor_note: Use generic process labels and no job titles.

scenes.title: Prevent seller remorse by listening
scenes.spoken_text: Sometimes a seller signs and then starts second-guessing the decision. That often traces back to a concern that never felt heard. Maybe the seller worried about the move and everyone kept talking about price. Before agreement, ask what still feels unsettled and say the concern back in plain words. After agreement, keep the record current and route new questions quickly. Listening early is stronger than trying to calm a preventable surprise later.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea listening beside a seller with an unresolved move card
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the seller thoughtful rather than distressed.

scenes.title: Identify closing risks early
scenes.spoken_text: Other problems can surface late. A family member may question the decision because they never heard the facts. A lien or unclear ownership may interrupt the closing review. Another buyer may appear with different terms. You cannot remove every risk, but you can ask about decision makers, ownership, notices, and competing options during discovery. Accurate notes and early escalation give the current process time to address what is real. When a new issue appears, update the seller rather than disappearing while it is reviewed. Clear communication keeps an open question from becoming a new reason to doubt the whole process.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside family, title, and competing-option risk cards
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no alarm icons or guaranteed resolution.

scenes.title: Continue to the capstone
scenes.spoken_text: Take the Closing and Deal Engineering checkpoint next. Then complete the Cadence and Close Capstone.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea centered in a medium shot

edit_operations.source_problem: Role-bound narration and labels appear throughout the lesson spine. The full recut preserves all offer preparation, presentation, consent, risk, and logistics teaching while making ownership reusable and role agnostic.
